relatedProblems.133: Graph deep copy: use a hash map to track visited nodes and recursively/BFS clone the entire undirected graph
relatedProblems.207: Directed graph cycle detection: if prerequisite courses form a cycle, completion is impossible — use topological sort to check DAG property
relatedProblems.210: Topological sort: find a linear learning order in a DAG using Kahn's algorithm or DFS
relatedProblems.684: Cycle detection / Union-Find: find the extra edge that creates a cycle in an undirected tree
relatedProblems.269: Advanced topological sort: derive letter ordering from dictionary sequence, build graph and output a valid alphabet order

sections.heading: Core Concepts
sections.content: A graph is built from **vertices** and **edges**, modeling [[primary|binary relationships]] between entities — a [[primary|data structure]] often compared to a road network or a social network.
items: **Directed vs. undirected**: an **edge** may have direction — a directed graph captures relationships with [[primary|ordering]], while an undirected graph is bidirectional
**Weight**: a numeric value on an **edge**, often representing distance or cost — the foundation for [[primary|shortest-path]] algorithms
**Degree**: the number of **edges** incident to a vertex; in a directed graph it splits into [[primary|in-degree]] (incoming) and [[primary|out-degree]] (outgoing)

**Path and cycle**: a closed path whose start and end are the same vertex is a [[warning|cycle]], and a graph with no cycles is **acyclic**; a connected undirected graph with V vertices and ≥ V edges must contain a cycle

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Graph operation costs (let `n` be the number of vertices) depend on the underlying storage: an **adjacency list** is best for frequent insertions and deletions, while an **adjacency matrix** excels at fast connectivity lookups.
items: **Add vertex**: [[success|O(1)]] for an adjacency list; [[danger|O(n²)]] for an adjacency matrix because the matrix must be resized
**Add edge**: [[success|O(1)]] in both representations — the position is directly addressable
**Check adjacency**: [[success|O(1)]] index lookup in an adjacency matrix; up to [[warning|O(n)]] worst case in an adjacency list, since neighbors must be scanned

sections.heading: What It Optimizes
sections.content: Graphs excel at expressing **complex relationships** and network paths, but are overkill for purely linear or hierarchical data. If the data has only a single parent-child structure, a **tree** is more efficient and avoids the overhead of handling cycles.
items: **Tune storage to density**: a **sparse graph** saves space with an **adjacency list**; a **dense graph** gets faster adjacency queries from an **adjacency matrix**
**Optimize connectivity checks**: if you only need to know whether two nodes are in the same group (no full path required), [[primary|Disjoint-set (Union-Find)]] is dramatically faster
**Tune search to weights**: use **BFS** for unweighted graphs; **Dijkstra** for non-negative weights; switch to **Bellman-Ford** when negative weights are possible

sections.heading: When to Use It
sections.content: Whenever a problem involves "network connections," "path search," or "dependency order" between entities, a graph is the right fit. Abstract the problem into vertices and edges, and a standard algorithm usually applies.
items: **Shortest-path problems**: map navigation, network routing, or word-ladder problems that minimize transformation steps
**Topological sort**: tasks with precedence and dependencies, such as project scheduling or spreadsheet recalculation
**Minimum spanning tree**: laying network cabling or broadcasting information while connecting all nodes at the lowest total weight
**Maze and connectivity**: six degrees of separation in social networks, or the classic maze-exit search via graph traversal

sections.heading: Common Graph Representations
sections.content: Beginners often struggle to turn abstract vertices and edges into real code. Calling out the implementation differences between the [[primary|adjacency matrix]] and the [[primary|adjacency list]] helps bridge the gap from theory to practice.
items: **Adjacency matrix**: implemented as a **2D array**, ideal for **dense graphs** — extremely fast adjacency checks, but consumes [[danger|O(V²)]] space; for an **undirected graph** the matrix is always a [[primary|symmetric matrix]]
**Adjacency list**: stores neighbors in a **list** per vertex, ideal for **sparse graphs** — fast operations with only [[success|O(V+E)]] space
**From theory to code**: most real-world graphs are **sparse**, so the **adjacency list** is the most common way to map abstract relationships into actual code

sections.heading: Classic Graph Algorithms
sections.content: A graph's power comes entirely from its dedicated algorithms. Listing the core ones gives beginners a clear [[primary|follow-up learning roadmap]] for applying graph theory to real problems.
items: **Graph traversal**: level-by-level **BFS** and deep-first **DFS** are the foundation of graph theory — both run in [[success|O(V+E)]]
**Shortest path**: minimize cost between two nodes — **Dijkstra** for non-negative weights, **Bellman-Ford** when negative weights exist
**Minimum spanning tree**: connect all vertices with minimum total weight — **Kruskal** (paired with Disjoint-set) or **Prim**
**Topological sort**: handles task dependencies in a **directed acyclic graph (DAG)**, turning a tangled precedence relation into a linear sequence
